text: The surgeon visually examines the surgical field, identifying a large indirect hernia defect in the right lower quadrant.
The surgeon creates a wide peritoneal flap, revealing the hernia defect. The defect is located in the indirect space, and the surgeon decides not to close it to avoid nerve entrapment.
The surgeon identifies a true obturator hernia on the patient's side, characterized by a visible peritoneal sac. The hernia is planned to be covered with mesh, alongside a small fat plug.
The surgeon reduces the entire hernia sac, with the round ligament visibly present in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully dissects the peritoneum away from the underlying structures to safely cauterize and divide it, completing the parietalization of the right medial preperitoneal space.

The surgeon examines the obturator space, identifying a hernia defect with a visible peritoneal sac extending into the area. The surgeon prepares to reduce the hernia, noting the presence of a true peritoneal sac rather than a typical fat plug.
The surgeon completes the preparation of the surgical site, creating adequate space for the placement of the mesh to cover the hernia defect.
The surgeon checks for a lipoma in the indirect space, observing a small amount of fat. The entire hernia defect is visible, appearing as a widened indirect ring.
The surgeon places a fifteen by fifteen pro grip mesh over the hernia defect, ensuring that the obturator space is adequately covered.
The surgeon proceeds to close the flap over the surgical area, completing the coverage of the hernia repair site.
